questions.explanation: A block hash commits to the block’s contents (including the previous block hash), creating an integrity chain where changes become detectable.

options.A: To encrypt all transaction data so only miners can read it
options.B: To link blocks and make tampering evident by committing to the block’s contents
options.C: To generate new private keys for users on the network
options.D: To compress the block so it uses less storage

questions.question: In a typical blockchain, what is the primary purpose of a cryptographic hash in each block?

questions.explanation: The EVM is the deterministic execution environment for smart contract bytecode, ensuring all nodes reach the same result.

options.A: A centralized server that stores all Ethereum account passwords
options.B: A peer-to-peer file system used to host dApps
options.C: A deterministic runtime environment that executes smart contract bytecode on Ethereum nodes
options.D: A database that only validators can query

questions.question: Which statement best describes the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)?

questions.explanation: ERC-721 defines a standard interface for unique, non-fungible tokens.
questions.question: Which token standard is primarily used for non-fungible tokens (NFTs) where each token is unique?

questions.explanation: State variables marked public get an automatically generated getter function.
questions.question: In Solidity, which keyword makes a state variable accessible via an auto-generated getter function?

questions.explanation: Merkle trees support compact proofs (Merkle proofs) that a transaction is included in a block, enabling light clients and efficient verification.

options.A: They allow nodes to execute smart contracts faster by parallelizing opcodes
options.B: They enable efficient inclusion proofs for transactions without downloading the entire block
options.C: They replace digital signatures for transaction authorization
options.D: They prevent front-running by hiding transaction contents

questions.question: What is the main reason Merkle trees are used in blockchains?

questions.explanation: Mainnet forking lets you run tests locally while reading real mainnet state at a chosen block height.

options.A: Use a local in-memory chain with no forking and manually mock all external contracts
options.B: Use mainnet forking in Hardhat/Foundry to simulate mainnet state locally
options.C: Deploy to mainnet and run tests using small-value transactions
options.D: Only run static analysis; runtime tests are unnecessary

questions.question: You want to test your contract against real mainnet state (e.g., existing ERC-20 balances) without deploying to mainnet. Which approach is most appropriate?

questions.explanation: A Signer holds the ability to sign transactions; a Provider alone can read chain data but cannot authorize state changes.
questions.question: In ethers.js, which object is typically required to send a state-changing transaction (e.g., calling a non-view function) from a specific account?

questions.explanation: delegatecall runs the callee’s code in the caller’s storage context; a malicious/buggy implementation can corrupt storage (e.g., ownership) and seize control.

options.A: The plugin cannot read storage from the calling contract, so state will be lost
options.B: The plugin executes in the caller’s context and can overwrite storage, enabling full takeover if the plugin is malicious or compromised
options.C: The plugin will always run out of gas because delegatecall doubles gas costs
options.D: delegatecall automatically prevents reentrancy, so the main risk is only DoS

questions.question: A contract uses `delegatecall` to a plugin address that can be updated by an admin. What is the most critical security risk in this design?

questions.explanation: Reading from storage (SLOAD) is expensive; caching the value in a local variable avoids repeated SLOADs inside the loop.

options.A: Replace `uint256` with `int256` to reduce opcode cost
options.B: Cache `feeBps` into a local memory variable once before the loop and use the cached value
options.C: Mark the function `payable` so it gets a larger gas stipend
options.D: Emit an event each iteration to reduce storage reads

questions.question: You are optimizing gas in a function that repeatedly reads the same storage variable `uint256 feeBps;` inside a loop. Which change most reliably reduces gas usage?

questions.explanation: ERC-1155 is a multi-token standard supporting many token IDs in one contract, including fungible and non-fungible patterns, with batch transfers/mints.

options.A: ERC-20, because it supports both fungible and non-fungible tokens in one contract
options.B: ERC-721, because it supports batch minting and fungible balances
options.C: ERC-1155, because it supports multiple token IDs (fungible and non-fungible) with batch operations
options.D: ERC-777, because it replaces the need for NFTs

questions.question: You are building an on-chain game that needs both fungible in-game currency and many item types, some fungible (e.g., potions) and some non-fungible (e.g., unique swords). Which token standard best fits to minimize deployments and support batch transfers?
